coredata.dc:title: Wavelength-sensitive photocatalytic H2 evolution from H2S splitting over g-C3N4 with S,N-codoped carbon dots as the photosensitizer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. and Science Press. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. and Science Press.
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Zhanghui
Yu, Shan
Fan, Xiang-Bing
Wei, Shiqian
Yu, Limei
Zhong, Yunqian
Gao, Xue-Wang
Wu, Fan
Zhou, Ying
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic splitting of hydrogen sulfide (H2S) for hydrogen evolution is a promising method to solve the energy and environmental issues. In this work, S,N-codoped carbon dots (S,N-CDs)/graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheet is synthesized by hydrothermal method as an efficient photocatalyst for the decomposition of H2S. In addition to the characterization of the morphology and structure, chemical state, optical and electrochemical performances of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4, hydrogen evolution tests show that the activity of g-C3N4 is improved by introducing S,N-CDs, and the enhancement depends strongly on the wavelength of incident light. The photocatalytic hydrogen production rate of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite reaches 832 μmol g−1h−1, which is 38 times to that of g-C3N4 under irradiation at 460 nm. Density functional theory calculations and electron paramagnetic resonance as well as photoluminescence technologies have altogether authenticated that the unique wavelength-dependent photosensitization of S,N-CDs on g-C3N4; meanwhile, a good match between the energy level of S,N-CDs and g-C3N4 is pivotal for the effective photocatalytic activity. Our work has unveiled the detailed mechanism of the photocatalytic activity enhancement in S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite and showed its potential in photocatalytic splitting of H2S for hydrogen evolution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photosensitization
S,N-codoped carbon dots
Hydrogen sulfide splitting
Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic splitting of H2S for hydrogen evolution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the naked g-C3N4 (purple line), S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 (cyan line) and pristine S,N-CDs (green line) samples.
(a) TEM image of g-C3N4, (b) HRTEM image and (c) depicts of lattice spacing of the S,N-CDs, (d, e) TEM and (f) SAED images of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the pure g-C3N4 and S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite. Insets show the enlarged absorption spectroscopy of the composite, and digital photograph of g-C3N4 and S,N-CDs/g-C3N4.
XPS (a) survey spectra, high-resolution spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) O 1s for S,N-CDs, g-C3N4 and S,N-CDs/g-C3N4.

The photocatalytic H2 evolution rate from H2S splitting for the as prepared g-C3N4 and S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 under different conditions. (a) control experiments under visible-light irradiation (LED lamp, λ = 460 nm, 150 mW/cm2); (b) S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 with different loading amount of S,N-CDs (LED lamp, λ = 460 nm, 150 mW/cm2); (c) g-C3N4 and S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 (300 W Xe lamp, λ > 420 nm, 600 mW/cm2 and (d) g-C3N4 and S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 under irradiation by three LED lamps (λ = 395 nm, 160 mW/cm2; λ = 460 nm, 150 mW/cm2 and λ = 525 nm, 150 mW/cm2; (e) S,N-CDs/g-C3N4, Pt/g-C3N4 and Pt-S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 (1wt%), and (f) long-term experiment of Pt-S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 (LED lamp, λ = 460 nm, 150 mW/cm2). Experiment conditions: 7.5 mmol H2S (theoretical amount) in 5 mL Na2SO3-Na2S (0.6 M−0.1 M); catalyst, 1 mg.
(a) The photocurrent and (b) electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) Nyquist plot of g-C3N4 and S,N-CDs/g-C3N4.
The differential charge distribution of interface for (a) S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 and (b) CDs/g-C3N4 samples. Cyan represents the electron emergence and the yellow means the electron missing (0.006 e/Å3).
(a) The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra of g-C3N4, and (b) of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 under different wavelengths of LED lamp (λ = 525, 460, and 395 nm).

(a–c) Steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra of S,N-CDs with concentration of 0.025 mg/mL with different excitation wavelengths in the range of 380–410, 450–480 and 500–560 nm, respectively (excitation wavelength interval 10 or 20 nm); (d) the PL excitation (PLE) spectra of S,N-CDs with the emission wavelength at 460, 537, and 613 nm.

(a) The energy level diagram of S,N-CDs with different excitation wavelength regions; (b–d) the schematic illustration of the possible photocatalytic processes in S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite under three cases of irradiation at about λ = 395, 460 and 525 nm.

head.ce:title: Wavelength-sensitive photocatalytic H2 evolution from H2S splitting over g-C3N4 with S,N-codoped carbon dots as the photosensitizer

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Oil and Gas Reservoir Geology and Exploitation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Activity enhancement of S,N codoped carbon dots (S,N-CDs) on g-C3N4 for photocatalytic H2 evolution from H2S splitting is heavily dependent on the wavelength of the incident light due to their dominant photosensitization effect.

Photocatalytic splitting of hydrogen sulfide (H2S) for hydrogen evolution is a promising method to solve the energy and environmental issues. In this work, S,N-codoped carbon dots (S,N-CDs)/graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheet is synthesized by hydrothermal method as an efficient photocatalyst for the decomposition of H2S. In addition to the characterization of the morphology and structure, chemical state, optical and electrochemical performances of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4, hydrogen evolution tests show that the activity of g-C3N4 is improved by introducing S,N-CDs, and the enhancement depends strongly on the wavelength of incident light. The photocatalytic hydrogen production rate of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite reaches 832 μmol g−1h−1, which is 38 times to that of g-C3N4 under irradiation at 460 nm. Density functional theory calculations and electron paramagnetic resonance as well as photoluminescence technologies have altogether authenticated that the unique wavelength-dependent photosensitization of S,N-CDs on g-C3N4; meanwhile, a good match between the energy level of S,N-CDs and g-C3N4 is pivotal for the effective photocatalytic activity. Our work has unveiled the detailed mechanism of the photocatalytic activity enhancement in S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite and showed its potential in photocatalytic splitting of H2S for hydrogen evolution.

ce:section.ce:para:
Conversion of solar energy into hydrogen energy is a promising way to meet the increasing energy demand worldwide [1–4]. However, due to the large Gibbs energy changes required (ΔG = 284.7 kJ mol−1), direct hydrogen evolution from overall water splitting is very challenging, and only a few reports have been raised with limited efficiency [5–8]. Hydrogen sulfide (H2S), as a hazardous pollutant gas, which is abundant in oil and gas reservoirs, can be used as the alternative resource to produce hydrogen easily from the thermodynamic point of view (ΔG = 33.7 kJ mol−1). Solar-driven processes such as photoelectrochemical and photocatalytic methods have received special interests in these years as they can not only decompose the dangerous H2S gas, but also provide efficient ways for hydrogen generation [9–15]. For example, Li et al. in 2008 utilized CdS-based photocatalyst to decompose H2S and realized efficient hydrogen generation using ethanolamine as the reaction medium [16]. Recently, we also designed multiple solid solution or heterojunction photocatalysts such as MnS/(In x Cu1−
x )2S3 [17], MnS/In2S3 [18], MnS/In2S3/PdS [19], In2S3/CuS [20] and Cd x In1−
x S [21] for efficient hydrogen evolution from H2S splitting in the solution of Na2S and Na2SO3. From the perspective of the design of photocatalysts, low-cost and low-toxicity carbon materials can provide a new choice of photocatalysts for the photocatalytic H2 evolution from H2S splitting in addition to metal-based photocatalysts.

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) as a nonmetallic semiconductor photocatalyst has gained tremendous interests since its pioneering work in 2009 due to the advantages of good stability, favorable band structure and facile preparation [22]. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 is dramatically restrained on account of the insufficient visible light absorption and serious recombination of photogenerated charge carriers [23,24]. In order to address this issue, various strategies including morphology control [25–28], doping with metal/nonmetal elements [29–32] and coupling with other materials [33–37] have been proposed.

Among the methods above, coupling g-C3N4 with carbon dots (CDs), the zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with less than 10 nm, is regarded as one of the most popular ways to promote the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 [38–40]. Diversified researches about the composite of CDs and g-C3N4 have been reported in photocatalysis such as hydrogen evolution [41], CO2 reduction, dyes and antibiotics degradation [42,43]. However, the mechanism behind the enhanced activity of CDs/g-C3N4 is often vaguely attributed to the synergistic effects of photosensitization, improved charge separation as well as upconversion fluorescence effect from CDs, and in-depth study is very limited. Very recently, Zhu et al. reported the wavelength-dependent activity of CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid from the point view of reaction kinetics for degradation of organic contaminant, demonstrating the dependence of the photocatalytic efficiency on the wavelength of incident light [44]. Nevertheless, it is still necessary to disclose the fundamental impact of CDs on g-C3N4 from the hybrid itself for the enhancement of photocatalytic activity.

Herein, we have fabricated composite of g-C3N4 and S, N codoped CDs (S,N-CDs) for the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution from H2S. The amount of hydrogen produced by S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite is 38 times to that of g-C3N4 with optimized conditions. Importantly, it is discovered that the distinct enhancement of the photocatalytic activity of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 depends heavily on the wavelength of irradiation light. In-depth studies by energy level analysis of S,N-CDs and density functional theory (DFT) calculation indicate that photosensitization effect from S,N-CDs, rather than the charge separation effect, plays the dominant role in promoting the activity of the system. In addition, the favorable match of the different energy level between S,N-CDs and g-C3N4 is also necessary for effective hydrogen evolution from H2S splitting.

Citric acid (C6H8O7), thiourea (CH4N2S), ethanol (C2H5OH), sodium sulfide (Na2S·9H2O), potassium hydroxide (KOH) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Chron Chemicals (Chengdu, China). N,N-Dimethyl formamide (DMF) was employed through Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Urea (CH4N2O) was obtained from Kermel (Chengdu, China). All chemical reagents were used directly without purification.

S,N-CDs were synthesized according to the solvothermal method proposed by Qu et al. [45]. Briefly, 2 g (26.3 mmol) thiourea and 1 g (5.2 mmol) citric acid were dispersed into 10 mL DMF followed by stirring to from a uniform solution. The obtained solution was then poured into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and held at 160 °C for 6 h. After cooling naturally, 10 mL KOH solution (100 mg/mL) was poured into the system under magnetic stirring for 5 min, and then the solution was subjected to centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 15 min. The precipitate was further washed with deionized (DI) water and collected by centrifugation. After that, the product was dialyzed in DI water for 48 h for removal of water-soluble impurities, and S,N-CDs were obtained by freeze-drying. In addition, we prepared N-doped carbon dots (NCD) by replacing thiourea with urea under the same experimental conditions, and carbon dots without heteroatoms (CDs) by discarding thiourea and replacing DMF with DI water.

g-C3N4 was obtained by the thermal polymerization method [46]. Typically, the precursor urea was calcined in ceramic container at 550 °C for 60 min. The naturally cooled product with light yellow color was g-C3N4.

S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite was prepared via a facile hydrothermal method. First, 100 mg g-C3N4 was dispersed into 20 mL DI water for sonication, and x wt% S,N-CDs (compared to g-C3N4, x = 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 10) were added into the suspension of g-C3N4 under magnetic stirring until the color of suspension solution became dark purple. Subsequently, the mixture solution was added into a 50 mL autoclave and maintained at 120 °C for 2 h. Afterwards, the mixture was cooled down naturally for centrifugation. Finally, the product was dried at 60 °C overnight. In the absence of special instructions, S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 in this work refers to 3 wt% S,N-CDs/g-C3N4. Note that g-C3N4 samples were also treated with the above hydrothermal method for characterizations and experiments. In addition, we prepared 3 wt% CDs/g-C3N4 and 3 wt% NCDs/g-C3N4 under the same experimental conditions.

1 wt% metallic Pt was loaded on S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 by photo-deposition method. Specially, 100 mg sample was introduced into 50 mL DI water, then 1.2 mL isopropyl alcohol and 125 μL H2PtCl6 (16.8 mg/mL) were added into the suspension. Subsequently, the obtained suspension was kept for 30 min under ultrasound in inert environment, and then irradiated by Xe lamp for 2 h under stirring. Afterwards, the product was subjected to centrifugation repeatedly with 9000 rpm for 2 min and washed several times with DI water, and finally collected by vacuum drying.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was recorded on JEOL JSM-7800F microscope. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was taken on FEI TECNAI G2S-TWIN microscope. Powder X-ray diffraction patterns were conducted on PANalytical X’pert using Cu K α radiation at 40 kV/40 mA. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of prepared samples were examined with Thermo Nicolet 360 FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was taken on Thermo ESCALAB250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer and the binding energy of the samples was further calibrated with the C 1s peak at 284.8 eV. The N2 sorption isotherm and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area were evaluated by the nitrogen adsorption method (QuadraSorb Station 1 version5.04). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra and UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were obtained by Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer and Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer, respectively. The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was measured via the Bruker E500 spectrometer operating with different LED lamps at room temperature.

Photoelectrochemical measurements were tested on CHI660E electrochemical work station (Chenhua Instrument, Shanghai, China) in a standard three-electrode system with Pt wire employing as the counter electrode and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. The working electrodes were obtained by coating slurries of samples dispersed in dimethyl formamide (DMF) over an ITO glass substrate with an active area of ca. 0.5 cm2 and dried under vacuum at 60 °C overnight. The photocurrent measurement and the electrochemical impedance test were performed using 0.5 M Na2SO4 and 0.6 M Na2SO3 as electrolytes, respectively. A 300 W Xe lamp was served as the light source. The transient photocurrent at open circuit potential was tested under chopped illumination with a switching time of 100 s.

The photocatalytic activity measurement was conducted on a home-made setup [13]. Firstly, the aqueous solution (50 mL) with 0.1 M Na2S and 0.6 M Na2SO3 was added into a Pyrex flask (50 mL), which was bubbled with Ar for 20 min. After that, 0.075 mol Na2S solution (150 mL) was slowly added into 0.15 mol HCl solution (150 mL) at the rate of 1 mL/min to generate H2S gas, which was continuously bubbled into Pyrex flask to form the H2S solution. Meanwhile, 1 mg photocatalyst was added into the photoreactor under magnetic stirring. Then 5 mL H2S solution was transferred from the Pyrex flask into the photoreactor (10 mL quartz tube) via a syringe, and rinsed with argon for 5 min to remove the dissolved oxygen. After that, 0.5 mL CH4 gas was injected into the photoreactor as an internal standard for quantitative analysis of H2, and then the photoreactor was irradiated by 3 W monochromatic LED lamps (at 395, 460 and 525 nm) and 300 W Xe lamp according to the experimental requirements. The evolved hydrogen was determined using TM GC-2010 Plus gas chromatograph (argon carrier gas, TCD detector and molecular sieve 5 Å).

Density functional theory (DFT) calculation was conducted to simulate the differential charge density of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite with CASTEP method. The structural relaxation calculations were treated with Generalized Gradient Approximation (GGA) in the form of Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE). In order to optimize the geometry, all forces acting on the atoms were converged to less than 0.05 eV/Å; the total stress tensor was reduced to 0.1 GPa; the maximum ionic displacement was within 0.002 Å; and the energy cutoff of 380 eV and a 2 × 2 × 1 k-point of the atomic orbitals were used for the static calculations.

The as-synthesized samples were studied by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) and the patterns are displayed in Fig. 1 . For g-C3N4, two major peaks at 13.0° and 27.4° corresponds to its (1 0 0) and (0 0 2) facets. Specifically, the (1 0 0) facet is related to the in plane structural packing of the oriented melons and (0 0 2) facet is related to a characteristic interplanar stacking of the aromatic systems [47]. Notably, it indicates that the graphitized structure of the pristine S,N-CDs is similar to the prepared g-C3N4 from the overlapped diffraction peak at 27.4°. Furthermore, the diffraction peaks of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite show little change compared with g-C3N4, and this is indicative of that the intrinsic structure of g-C3N4 is maintained after the introduction of S,N-CDs. As a result, S,N-CDs are probably modified on the surface of g-C3N4 in our system [41].

The microscopic morphologies of g-C3N4, S,N-CDs, and S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 are characterized by TEM and HRTEM (Fig. 2 ). g-C3N4 exhibits as thin nanosheets with large amount of porous structures (Fig. 2a) and chiffon-like curled wrinkles at the edges (Fig. S1), which is beneficial for its interaction with CDs as reported [25]. S,N-CDs have spherical structure with uniform size distribution at around 4 nm in Fig. 2(b). Moreover, the image with high resolution shows the lattice fringe of 3.2 Å, which is ascribed to lattice spacing of the (0 0 2) facet of S,N-CDs (Fig. 2c). As illustrated in Fig. 2(d, e), TEM images of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 reveal that the porous structure of g-C3N4 is well kept and S,N-CDs are uniformly distributed on g-C3N4. In addition, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) image (Fig. 2f) manifests that S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite has two distinct diffraction rings ascribed to the (1 0 0) and (0 0 2) crystal planes of g-C3N4, which is consistent with the XRD results.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy is utilized to identify the light absorption ability of the samples. The light absorption of S,N-CDs is more distinct than that of NCDs and CDs (Fig S2), indicating that S and N doping significantly improves the visible light absorption of CDs. The corresponding light absorption enhancement of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite is in step with the absorption of S,N-CDs compared with pure g-C3N4 (Figs. 3 and S2). Actually, the visible light response of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 gradually increases with the augment of feed ratio of S,N-CDs (Fig. S3). In addition, photographs in the inset of Fig. 3 show that the color of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 is obviously different from that of g-C3N4. According to the above analysis, S,N-CDs substantially improve the light response of the composite, which is important for the promotion of photocatalytic performance. Furthermore, S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite shows two absorption bands centered at 457 and 567 nm as depicted in Fig. S2 and inset of Fig. 3, which may be corresponding to the n-π* transition of the conjugated CN and CS bonds [48].

Chemical states of elements in g-C3N4, S,N-CDs and S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite are analyzed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements. The survey spectra mainly show three peaks at 283.6, 397.7 and 530.0 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s of all the samples respectively (Fig. 4 a). Furthermore, the weak peaks of S,N-CDs at 163.5 eV (C–S) and 168.5 eV (C–SO3) are originated from S (4.5 wt%, Table S1 and Fig. S4) [49], which are however not detectable in the composite due to the small loading amount of S,N-CDs (only 0.5 wt% of S detected as shown in Table S1). The spectrum of C 1s of S,N-CDs (Fig. 4b) shows three distinctive peaks at 288.0, 286.1 and 284.8 eV, which correspond to CO/CN, C–S/C–N and sp 2 C–C/CC (π conjugate structures), respectively [50,51]. Meanwhile, a predominant graphitic peak at 288.3 eV, corresponding to C–N–C in S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 and g-C3N4, indicates that S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composites have the same structure as g-C3N4 [52]. In addition, two peaks of S,N-CDs located at 286.3 and 284.8 eV are also maintained in the spectrum of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite. The N 1s spectrum of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 shows peaks related to N–(C)3 at 400.2 eV and CN–C at 398.7 eV from g-C3N4, as well as graphitic N (401.6 eV), pyrrolic N (399.8 eV) and pyridinic N (397.9 eV) from S,N-CDs (Fig. 4c) [53]. The peaks centered at 531.7 and 533.3 eV suggest the existence of CO bonds and hydroxyl groups in S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 [54], which are similar with g-C3N4 and S,N-CDs (Fig. 4d).

Besides, functional groups on S,N-CDs, g-C3N4, and S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 samples are determined by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. S5). Peaks of S,N-CDs at 713, 1050, 1659 and 3193 cm−1 are contributed to C–S, CS, CC and N–H bonds [48]. g-C3N4 exhibits typical stretching vibrations at 808 (C–N), 1320 (triazine units), 1573 (CC), 1659 (CO) and 3193 cm−1 (N–H) [48,55]. In addition to absorption peaks relevant to g-C3N4, the asymmetric stretching vibration of CS at 1050 cm−1 in the spectrum of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite indicates the existence of S,N-CDs.

All the above results verify that S,N-CDs are effectively anchored on the surface of g-C3N4, which may result from several chemical interactions: (i) π-π stacking effect between S,N-CDs and g-C3N4 due to the coexistence of π conjugate structures [56–58]; (ii) chemical interaction of sulfonic groups from S,N-CDs and residual amine groups from g-C3N4 [49]; (iii) hydrogen bond formed by hydroxyl groups and amine groups in S,N-CDs and g-C3N4 [40,59].

N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms measurement is then performed to test BET surface area and the pore structure of all samples (Fig. S6). It is found that the N2 adsorption isotherms of g-C3N4 and S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 are both categorized as type IV with H3 hysteresis loop, which confirms the mesoporous structure of g-C3N4 in consistent with TEM results. The BET surface area of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composites (41.7 m2/g) is similar to that of g-C3N4 (44.2 m2/g), certifying that the slight change of surface area is not the key factor affecting the photocatalytic activity of the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic splitting of H2S for hydrogen evolution
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows the photocatalytic H2 evolution results from H2S splitting with a series of samples. Control experiment shows that no H2 is evolved in the absence of either light irradiation or photocatalysts, confirming the process of H2S splitting is based on photocatalysis (Fig. 5a). S,N-CDs alone is ineffective for H2 evolution while bare g-C3N4 can slightly generate hydrogen. Inspiringly, the amount of H2 generated by S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 is promoted significantly compared to g-C3N4. Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 with different weight ratio of S,N-CDs is also explored (Fig. 5b). All composites generate much more amount of H2 than g-C3N4, confirming the positive effect of S,N-CDs on g-C3N4. The rate of H2 evolution decreases when the amount of S,N-CDs is larger than 3 wt% in the composite, which is possibly resulted from the block of reaction active sites by excessive amount of S,N-CDs.

To further identify the efficiency of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution, different light sources are adopted for the test. Fig. 5(c) shows that the H2 generation rate of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 is improved by 17 times (from 12 to 203 μmol g−1h−1) in contrast to pure g-C3N4 under visible light irradiation by Xe lamp (λ > 420 nm). Besides, photocatalytic splitting of H2S has also been carried out using LED lamps with different wavelengths as the light source (Fig. 5d). Interestingly, the amount of H2 evolved by S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 is the highest under irradiation at 460 nm, which has increased 38 times from 22 to 832 μmol g−1h−1 compared with pure g-C3N4. In contrast, the rate of hydrogen evolution by S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 has increased only 1.8 times from 198 to 360 μmol g−1h−1 at 395 nm. A hydrogen evolution rate of 136 μmol g−1h−1 is reached by S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 with the light illumination at 525 nm, where no hydrogen is generated from g-C3N4. It is surprising that the apparent quantum yield (AQY) at different wavelengths is almost irrelevant to the DRS data of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 (Fig. S7). Accordingly, further thorough mechanism study behind the enhancement of photocatalytic activity of the composite should be carried out.

Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of the system is remarkably improved by deposition of Pt as cocatalyst on S,N-CDs/g-C3N4. Fig. 5(e) shows that introduction of 1 wt% Pt in the system enhances the hydrogen production rate to 3617 μmol g−1 h−1 with a further promotion of 4.3 times, with an apparent quantum yield (AQY) of 3.47% at 460 nm. It is noted that the amount of hydrogen generated by Pt-S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 is also more than that of the sum of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 and Pt/g-C3N4, which clearly implies that the synergistic interaction between Pt and S,N-CDs promotes the photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4. Long-term H2 production test of Pt-S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 shows that the photocatalyst is stable within 10 h (Fig. 5f). Remarkably, the photocatalytic performance and AQY of the composite in our work is comparable or even higher to a number of reported CDs/g-C3N4 composites for photocatalytic H2 evolution (Table S2). Compared with other reports on metal-based photocatalysts in photocatalytic H2 evolution from H2S splitting (Table S3), the photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 is not as high as that of optimal metal-based photocatalysts such as CdS or MnS/In2S3 composite [16,18], but it is also significantly higher than that of some typical photocatalysts like TiO2 [60]. All the above results indicate that S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 as nonmetallic carbon materials have great potential for photocatalytic splitting of H2S and hydrogen evolution.

Photoelectrochemical measurements of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 and g-C3N4 are carried out to compare the amount of effective photogenerated charge carriers under visible light irradiation. Fig. 6 (a) shows that the transient photocurrent of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 has doubled over that of g-C3N4, and this demonstrates that more photogenerated charge carries in the composite are effectively collected. Meanwhile, electrochemical impedance spectra of the samples are also recorded in the presence or absence of visible light (Fig. 6b), in which the semicircular radius of the S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 is much smaller than that of g-C3N4. The results show that compared with g-C3N4, S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 has numerous photogenerated charge carriers that can migrate to external circuits. The above analyses are consistent with photocatalytic hydrogen evolution results and corroborate the importance of S,N-CDs in the composite.

Generally, it is considered that CDs can promote the photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4 from three main aspects: (i) Photosensitization effect to increase the light absorption in the region where g-C3N4 barely responses [61], (ii) charge separation effect to enhance the utilization efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers from g-C3N4 for photocatalytic reactions [62,63], and (iii) upconversion fluorescence effect to provide extra photons in the light-absorbing region of g-C3N4 [64,65]. In our system, the last effect can be excluded because no upconversion fluorescence with wavelength smaller than 440 nm (which corresponds to the light absorption region of g-C3N4) can be detected from S,N-CDs (even with large excitation and emission slit width during test). For photosensitization effect, electrons should mainly transfer from S,N-CDs to g-C3N4; contrarily, electrons should mainly transfer from g-C3N4 to S,N-CDs for charge separation effect [48–52].

DFT calculations are adopted to analyze the differential charge distribution of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite and it suggests that 0.2 electron will flow from S,N-CDs to g-C3N4 for each calculation unit shown in Fig. 7 (a). Therefore, it is reasonable to conclude that photosensitization effect of S,N-CDs on g-C3N4 in the composite is prevailing over the charge separation effect. Consequently, the activity of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 at ca. 460 nm should be higher than that at 395 nm due to the more notable photosensitization effect (see detailed analysis below). In addition, electron donating ability from CDs and NCDs to g-C3N4 is also studied, and 0.02 electron from CDs (Fig. 7b) and 0.14 electron from NCDs transfers to g-C3N4, respectively (Fig. S8). This demonstrates the importance of S and N doping of CDs in our system, which is further confirmed by the photocatalytic tests in Fig. S9 that S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 outperforms CDs/g-C3N4 and NCDs/g-C3N4 remarkably.

Nevertheless, it is intriguing that the photocatalytic performance of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 under irradiation at around 525 nm is much lower than that at 460 nm, which is clearly contrary to the DRS result of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 in Fig. 3. To further explore the photocatalytic mechanism of the system at different light wavelengths, electronic paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra of the samples are recorded. As depicted in Fig. 8 , S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 composite shows a stronger signal than pure g-C3N4, with only one single Lorentzian line centered at g = 2.0037, which is usually attributed to the unpaired electrons of carbon atoms in aromatic rings or π-π conjugates between the interface of S,N-CDs and g-C3N4 [66,67]. Notably, the signal intensity of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 at 460 nm is higher than that at 395 nm and 525 nm, which is similar to the photocatalytic result. This emphasizes the different influence of incident light wavelength on the interaction between S,N-CDs and g-C3N4 in photocatalysis.

To further reveal the interaction between S,N-CDs and g-C3N4, the energy level structure of S,N-CDs and g-C3N4 is investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. 9 (a–c), photoluminescence (PL) spectra of S,N-CDs present three stable emission peaks irrespective of the different excitation wavelengths, centered at ca. 460 nm (2.70 eV), 537 nm (2.31 eV) and 613 nm (2.02 eV). Moreover, PL excitation (PLE) spectra in Fig. 9(d) shows the three emission peaks mainly correspond to the excitation region of 380–410, 420–480 and 500–560 nm, respectively. Therefore, three different energy level states should coexisted in S,N-CDs, which probably originate from the π* orbital of the CO, CN, and CS bonds in the S,N-CDs [45,68]. Valence band (VB) XPS spectrum of S,N-CDs indicates that the HOMO energy level is ca. 1.79 eV (Fig. S10a). Accordingly, the energy level diagram of S,N-CDs is proposed in Fig. 10 (a). Meanwhile, in combination with the VB XPS spectrum and plots of (αhν)2 vs. hν (Fig. S10), CB position (−0.66 eV) and VB position (2.35 eV) of g-C3N4 can be obtained.

Based on the energy level structure of S,N-CDs and g-C3N4, we then have a clue on the different photocatalytic behavior of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 under irradiation with different wavelengths. Here, we speculate that hydrogen evolution should mainly occur on the g-C3N4 side of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 on account of almost no hydrogen evolution from S,N-CDs in the photocatalytic test (Fig. 5a). Under light illumination of 395 nm (Fig. 10b), both S,N-CDs and g-C3N4 can be excited. However, due to the smaller amount of S,N-CDs in the composite (3 wt%), the number of incident photons absorbed by S,N-CDs should be much less than that by g-C3N4. Consequently, only a small amount of electrons in S,N-CDs can be excited (to the energy level from −1.35 to −0.91 eV) and further transfer to g-C3N4. Accordingly, photosensitization effect at 395 nm is limited and this resulted in moderate enhancement of photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4. By contrast, under illumination at 460 nm (Fig. 10c), g-C3N4 is barely excited and this means that S,N-CDs can absorb much more incident light without competition. The photogenerated electrons at the corresponding energy level (−0.91 to −0.52 eV) of S,N-CDs then transfer to the CB of g-C3N4 and participate in the reduction of protons, which leads to significant improvement of hydrogen evolution efficiency in the system. Meanwhile, deposition of Pt on the composite can further accelerate the surface reaction kinetics and improve the efficiency of the system. Under illumination at 525 nm, S,N-CDs can absorb more incident light than that at 460 nm (Fig. 10d). Nevertheless, photogenerated electrons at the corresponding energy level (−0.57 to −0.23 eV) cannot effectively migrate to the CB of g-C3N4 (−0.66 eV) due to the inefficient thermodynamic driving force. As a result, only a few amounts of electrons participate in hydrogen evolution, which causes the low efficiency of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4.

In summary, S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposite has been successfully prepared and applied for photocatalytic H2 evolution from H2S splitting. The maximum H2 evolution rate of 3617 μmol g−1 h−1 is realized under irradiation at 460 nm. S,N-CDs mainly act as the photosensitizer to significantly enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of system. The effect of photosensitization largely depends on the wavelength of the irradiation light as well as the energy level match between S,N-CDs and g-C3N4. Accordingly, the photocatalytic activity of S,N-CDs/g-C3N4 is remarkably higher under illumination at 460 nm than that at 395 nm and 525 nm. This work provides a detailed perspective for the possible activity enhancement of the photocatalytic reactions by introduction of CDs, which potentially inspire people for more in-depth mechanism research on carbon dots-based composite systems.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
